description: RFC 8785 (JCS) numeric serialization conformance vectors. These pin the ECMAScript Number-to-String behavior that RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3 normatively requires: exponential-notation boundaries (>= 1e21 and <= 1e-7), the plain-decimal band, integer exactness through 2^53, negative-zero normalization, and IEEE 754 magnitude extremes. Implementations MUST reproduce these canonical forms and SHA-256 digests exactly; failure is non-conformance with RFC-ACDP-0001 §5.2.

vectors.name: JCS — exponential notation, large magnitude (RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3)
vectors.description: Numbers with magnitude >= 1e21 MUST serialize in exponential form with a '+' sign on the exponent and no leading zeros (e.g. 1e+21, not 1e21 or 1E21 or 1.0e+21). This is the ECMAScript Number-to-String algorithm RFC 8785 normatively references.
implementer_notes: RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3 defers number formatting to ECMAScript Number::toString. The exponent boundary for switching to exponential form on the high side is 1e21.
The exponent sign is always present for positive exponents ('e+21'), and the mantissa carries no trailing '.0'.
Implementations that emit '1e21' (no sign), '1E+21' (uppercase), or '1.0e+21' (trailing zero mantissa) are NON-conformant.

vectors.name: JCS — exponential notation, small magnitude (RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3)
vectors.description: Numbers with magnitude <= 1e-7 MUST serialize in exponential form with a '-' exponent (e.g. 1e-7). Just above the boundary (1e-6) stays in plain decimal form; see the decimal-band vector.
implementer_notes: The ECMAScript algorithm switches to exponential form on the low side at 1e-7: 0.000001 (1e-6) renders as decimal, 0.0000001 (1e-7) renders as '1e-7'.
Negative exponents carry the '-' sign with no leading zeros: 'e-7', not 'e-07'.

vectors.name: JCS — decimal band, no exponent (RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3)
vectors.description: Numbers whose magnitude is in [1e-6, 1e21) serialize in plain decimal notation with no exponent. Trailing fractional zeros are dropped and integers carry no decimal point.
implementer_notes: 0.000001 (1e-6) is the smallest magnitude that still renders in decimal form; anything smaller crosses into exponential notation.
Integral values inside the band render with no decimal point (100, not 100.0).

vectors.name: JCS — integer exactness through 2^53 (RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3)
vectors.description: Integers that are exactly representable as IEEE 754 doubles (|n| <= 2^53) serialize with their full integer digits and no decimal point or exponent. 2^53 = 9007199254740992.
implementer_notes: 2^53 (9007199254740992) is the largest consecutive integer exactly representable as a double; it must render in full, not as '9.007199254740992e15'.

Producers MUST NOT rely on exact JSON-number round-tripping for integer magnitudes above 2^53 — those values are not exactly representable as doubles and JCS canonicalization reflects the rounded double.

vectors.name: JCS — negative zero and trailing-zero normalization (RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3)
vectors.description: Negative zero (-0.0) canonicalizes to '0'; trailing fractional zeros are dropped (1.10 -> 1.1, 1.50 -> 1.5); positive zero stays '0'. This duplicates the can-001 number-formatting vector deliberately, as the most common conformance failure (stdlib json.dumps preserves -0.0).
implementer_notes: Python's stdlib json.dumps preserves '-0.0' and is therefore NON-conformant here; use the `jcs` PyPI library or pre-normalize negative zero.
Both -0.0 and 0.0 MUST produce the identical canonical token '0', so they hash identically.

vectors.name: JCS — IEEE 754 magnitude extremes (RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3)
vectors.description: The largest finite double (~1.7976931348623157e308) and the smallest positive subnormal double (5e-324) serialize via the same shortest-round-trip ECMAScript algorithm.
implementer_notes: These are boundary values of the IEEE 754 binary64 range; conformant JCS emits the shortest decimal string that round-trips to the same double.
NaN and +/-Infinity are NOT valid JSON numbers and MUST be rejected before canonicalization, not emitted in any form.
